12.4 Workers’ ID and Rights  Focus Questions:  When should international students apply for their SSN?  How much do international students have to be paid per hour?  Which employee rights and benefits are mandated by US law?  Do international students who earn income in the US have to pay US taxes?  Social Security Number (SSN)  Once the international student has been approved for employment in the US and starts working, they need a Social Security Number (SSN). Students should wait until they are in the US for at least 10 days and have reported to their educational institution, before applying for an SSN.  Students can visit their local Social Security Administration (SSA) office to apply for an SSN. The   Social Security Office Locator   will help to find the nearest office.  More information is available on the   Social Security website .  Workers’ Rights and Benefit  International students should be aware of their rights and benefits while working in the US.  US laws prohibit employers from discriminating against job seekers and employees because of their gender, race, country of origin, color, religion, or disability.  90% COMPLETE     Previous Quiz   Next Quiz     10/12/25, 4:22 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/12-4-workers-id-and-rights/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/12-4-workers-id-and-rights/   1/3
Workers have the right to be treated and paid fairly. International students should ensure they are paid at least the federal legal minimum wage, which is currently $7.25 per hour. If the minimum wage is higher in the state in which they work, they should be paid the higher amount. Employers are responsible for ensuring that the workplace is healthy and safe.  Some types of employee rights and benefits, such as minimum wage, overtime, leave under the Family Medical Leave Act, unemployment, and workers compensation and disability, are mandated under US law.  Other types of benefits, such as hazard pay, health care, maternity leave, and paid holidays, are not mandated by US law. They are provided at the discretion of the employer and will vary from job to job.  Deductions and Taxes  A student’s employer will deduct money from their paycheck for income tax, Social Security, and Medicare taxes. This is legal and done at all employers.  International students who earn income in the US must file a tax return with the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) by April 15 of the following year. For more information, go to   www.irs.gov .  General Tips  International students should:  Not work for any employer without signing a contract. Without this proof of employment, their rights may be severely reduced if anything goes wrong.  Always ask for pay stubs (a record of hours worked as well as taxes deducted and net pay) and keep them together in a safe place.  Check their pay stubs to ensure that their employer is deducting the necessary taxes from their pay. Not deducting taxes is illegal.  Not accept any “under the table” jobs where they are paid in cash and not registered as official employees. The US government takes working illegally very seriously. An international student caught working without permission may have to leave the US and not be allowed to return.  Not rely on earning extra money after their arrival in the US. Because the visa regulations regarding international student employment are fairly  10/12/25, 4:22 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/12-4-workers-id-and-rights/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/12-4-workers-id-and-rights/   2/3
restrictive, international students should plan their finances carefully and come prepared to meet their expenses without the need to work.     Previous Quiz   Back to Lesson   Next Quiz     10/12/25, 4:22 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/12-4-workers-id-and-rights/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/12-4-workers-id-and-rights/   3/3

L2. Working After Graduating  Graduates from the EU, European Economic Area or Switzerland can stay in France as long as they like after graduation to look for work.  Non-EU/EEA students on the VLS-TS   étudiant   visa graduating from French higher education institutions can work in France after completing their studies if they have a promise of employment in a job that pays at least 1.5 times the minimum wage. This applies to students graduating with bachelor’s, master’s, or doctoral degrees. Currently the minimum wage in France is EUR 10,03/hour gross or EUR 1525.47/month gross, which means students need to paid at least EUR 15.05/hour gross or EUR 2281.82/month to be able to stay on in France.  If they do not have a promise of employment, international students graduating with a master’s, professional bachelor’s degree   (Licence professionelle)   or post-graduate degree (e.g., a PhD) can apply for a non- renewable   one-year   Autorisation Provisoire de Séjour   (APS) temporary residence permit .   This permit   allows students to stay in France to look for a full-time job or to start a business.  A full list of degrees that qualify for the   recherche d’emploi/création d’entreprise   permit can be found   here .  Students with master’s and approved other degrees obtained at French higher education institutions who have left France after their studies to are permitted to apply for the   recherche d’emploi/création d’entreprise   permit  up to four years after graduation.  There are certain beneficial conditions (sometimes including being able to stay for longer than one year) attached to the   recherche d’emploi/création d’entreprise   permit permits for students from countries with bilateral  90% COMPLETE     Previous Topic   Next Quiz     10/12/25, 3:19 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/l2-working-after-graduating/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/l2-working-after-graduating/   1/2
agreements with France (e.g., Senegal, Gabon, Benin, Tunisia, Mauritius, Cape Verde, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, Macedonia, Montenegro, Serbia, Lebanon and India. Students from these countries should consult the website of the   Ministry of Internal Affairs   or contact the   Campus France Office   in their country for more information.  In addition, Indian students who have graduated with a master’s or PhD from a French higher education institution may be able to obtain a   post-study work visa   lasting up to five years.  While they seek full-time employment, students with a   recherche d’emploi/création d’entreprise ” permit can work in any job part-time (up to 60% of full-time hours). Those who find a job that pays at least 1.5 times the minimum wage can switch their immigration status from “student” to “employee” or “temporary worker.”  The temporary worker permit is valid for one year. At the end of that term, it is possible to request a “general, multi-annual residence permit for ‘employees’ for a period of four years.”  There are also work and immigration possibilities for certain students under the   “Talent Passport.”   Researchers who have been in France on this passport obtain a residency permit valid for the same period of time that was specified under their hosting agreement for up to four years. This process is renewable. Please visit the   Euraxess   website for more information.  Campus France has a   webpage   that students can consult for advice about looking for work while in France.     Previous Topic   Back to Lesson   Next Quiz     10/12/25, 3:19 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/l2-working-after-graduating/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/l2-working-after-graduating/   2/2

L. Working in France During and After Studies  ESSENTIAL KNOWLEDGE  Students on the VLS-TS visa can work part-time while studying, while students on the VLS-T (temporary visa) or in France for short-term programmes of less than a year cannot.  Students from the EU/EEA and Switzerland can work with no restrictions during and after studies.  After their studies, bachelor’s, master’s and doctoral students can stay in France if they receive an offer for a job that pays at least 1.5 times the minimum wage salary in France.  If they do not have a job offer, students with a master’s degree or other approved degree can apply for a residence permit allowing them to remain in France for a year to look for work or start a business. While they have this residence permit, they are permitted to work part-time (up to 60% of full-time hours).  Eventually there are opportunities to transition from “student” permits to “employee” permits allowing non-EU students meeting set conditions to remain in France to work and live.  90% COMPLETE     Previous Quiz   Next Topic     10/12/25, 3:19 AM   icef.com/academy/lessons/l-working-in-france-during-and-after-studies/  https://www.icef.com/academy/lessons/l-working-in-france-during-and-after-studies/   1/2

G2. Categories  While there is no definitive directory of   grandes écoles   – in fact, the term does not generally appear in the relevant French legislation or other official documents that codify the French system – there are some conventionally agreed categories or groupings that make it easier to understand this important pathway in French higher education. These can be broadly framed as follows.  Écoles normales supérieures   (ENS)  There are four such institutions currently, including the   École normale supérieure (Paris)   which is one of the country’s most prestigious and selective institutions. ENS Paris is a member-institution of the   Université Paris Sciences et Lettres , one of top-ranked French institutions in global university tables. It accepts just 200 new students each year through what can only be described as a highly competitive process. The é coles normales supérieures  emphasise studies in the sciences and humanities and are an important training ground for French scholars and civil servants.  Grandes écoles d’ingénieurs  The term “engineer” is very broadly defined within the French system and can be used to refer to conventional engineering disciplines and qualifications as well as to someone who has achieved an advanced level of more generalised study in a variety of fields, including science, the humanities and business administration.  There are somewhere between 150 and 200   grandes écoles d’ingénieurs , many of which are affiliated in regional or national groups of institutions. These include:  50% COMPLETE     Previous Topic   Next Topic     10/10/25, 5:11 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/g2-categories/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/g2-categories/   1/3
the   écoles nationales supérieures d’ingénieurs   (ENSI), a network of 40 advanced engineering schools throughout France;  the   Réseau Polytech , a group of 13 schools where each is in turn affiliated with a French university; and  the ParisTech group, which includes specialised institutes of aerospace and defense technology, agriculture and telecommunications. Also included in this latter group is the famous   École Polytechnique   (EP). Commonly known simply by the nickname “l’X”, the EP is one of the top- rated French institutions and also one of the most highly regarded engineering schools in the world.  Also included among the   grandes écoles d’ingénieurs   are the three  universités de technologie   (UT):   the   Université de technologie de Belfort- Montbéliard   (UTBM),   Université de technologie de Compiègne   (UTC) and the  Université de technologie de Troyes   (UTT). This is a distinct sub-category of institution which combines the selectivity and elite status of the   grandes écoles   with the research and technology transfer responsibilities of the universities.  Grandes écoles de commerce  Alongside the country’s leading engineering schools is the other major category of   grandes écoles de commerce . These elite business schools are mainly private institutions, in some cases operated by regional chambers of commerce in France. Unofficial counts suggest that there as many as 200  grandes écoles   for business studies, including the world-famous   Institut européen d’administration des affaires   (more commonly known as “INSEAD”), France’s leading graduate school of business and the home of the one of the most highly regarded MBA programmes in the world.  Other types of   grandes écoles  Outside of these major categories, there are also a number of   grandes écoles   dedicated to studies in political science and public administration. These include:  the   instituts d’études politiques   (IEP), a group of ten public, political science-focused institutions throughout the country; and  the   Institut d’études politiques de Paris , a Paris-based institution that is commonly referred to as “Sciences Po” and which has expanded in  10/10/25, 5:11 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/g2-categories/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/g2-categories/   2/3
recent years to include campuses in six other French cities.  Other specialist schools exist for advanced studies in journalism studies, art, architecture and social sciences.  Finally, there are three military academies that are also classified as  grandes écoles : the   École spéciale militaire de Saint-Cyr   (army), the   École de l’air   (air force) and the   École navale   (navy).     Previous Topic   Back to Lesson   Next Topic     10/10/25, 5:11 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/g2-categories/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/g2-categories/   3/3

D3. Homestay  Homestay refers to an international student living with a local Canadian family while studying in Canada. This is often one of the most memorable aspects of an international student’s experience. Being able to answer the questions an international student’s family will have about their child’s living arrangement is as important as knowing about the educational part of their study abroad experience.  International students living in homestay truly become a part of their Canadian host family and will be:  Integrated into all aspects of family life;  Fully immersed in the local culture;  Practising listening and speaking with native English and/or French speakers;  100% COMPLETE     Previous Quiz   Next Quiz     10/10/25, 4:57 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/d3-homestay/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/d3-homestay/   1/2
Cared for, supervised, and supported in all aspects of school/home life;  Provided with a private bedroom and three meals daily.  Canadian host families are well-screened. If qualified and selected, they are then eligible to be carefully matched with an international student. A home visit is conducted, the family is interviewed and reference checked, and a police/criminal background check is completed for all adults living in the home.  It is standard policy that only international students of different language/cultural backgrounds will live with the same host family.  The cost of homestay will vary in accordance with the cost of living in the region.  A Homestay Coordinator will be available 24 hours per day, seven days per week to address any questions or concerns that arise.  For specific details on the homestay options, the agent will have to liaise closely with the school the international student’s family is considering.  A few Canadian public school boards/divisions and more commonly independent or private schools offer international students the option to live in a boarding (dormitory) environment.  Copyright© 2012. E2. Levels of Study  As noted earlier, the French higher education system follows the LMD system ( Licence-Master-Doctorat,   or bachelor-master-doctoral).  Sub-degree qualifications are also available, notably the   Diplômes universitaires uechnologiques   (DUT). This nationally accredited diploma is awarded for students completing two years of post-secondary study, after which the student may enter the work force, continue to study for an additional year to earn a   Licence pro   degree (see below) or continue on to other university studies. There are two types of bachelor’s degrees in France: the   Licence professionelle ( more commonly referred to as the   Licence pro ) and the  Licence.   The first is a professional or vocational bachelor’s and the second is a traditional bachelor’s degree. These are completed in three years and degrees are awarded once 180 ECTS (under the European Credit Transfer System) have been obtained.  Licence pro   (three years)  Students enjoy the experience of an education delivered in close collaboration between universities and industry with a good deal of instruction provided by outside professionals. An internship is typically part of the experience. There are many fields of study offered at the   Licence pro  level including tourism and hospitality, agricultural and industrial production, health, environmental studies and art and design. Students must have completed two years of university study to be admitted to a  Licence pro   programme and then they study for one year in their chosen field to obtain their degree.  Campus France notes that “graduates of some professional   Licence pro  programmes are eligible to enroll directly in 1- or 2-year professional master’s programmes.” Apprenticeships are readily available to complement vocational training degrees and they span more than 450 trades.  For more information on the   Licence pro , please consult   this Campus France brief .  Licence   (three years)  This degree is a traditional bachelor’s degree with four study area options (known as   mentions ):  1.   Arts, languages and literature;  2.   Human and social sciences;  3.   Law, economics and management;  4.   Science, technology and health.  There are also two other degrees at the bachelor’s level: the DNA ( Diplôme national d’art, which is an   arts degree) and DEEA ( Diplôme d’études en  10/10/25, 5:01 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/e2-levels-of-study/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/e2-levels-of-study/   2/3
architecture , an architecture degree). These, like the   Licence   and   Licence pro,   are worth 180 ECT credits.  Master’s level (two years)  After they complete a   licence   students may apply to a master’s programme which they complete in two years (for a total of five years of post-secondary schooling including the three years devoted to the   licence   prior to the master’s level). The master’s degree is complete once 120 ECTS have been obtained, (300 in total with the   Licence   ECTs added in). There are vocational master’s programmes that prepare students to enter the workforce as well as academic ones that can lead to doctoral studies.  Doctorate level  Students may apply for a doctorate degree after having obtained a master’s degree. The doctorate involves six semesters divided into teaching and training components and students must complete a thesis that typically takes students three to four years to write. In the end, including the  Licence   and master’s degrees required to get into the doctorate, the doctorate is worth 480 ECTS credits and takes eight years to obtain. I3. The DUT  The Diplôme Universitaire de Technologie (DUT) is also a two-year national post-secondary diploma qualification. DUT options span 88 specialty fields which, as with the BTS, have been harmonised with the European LMD (Licence, Master, Doctorat) degree ladder.  As distinct from the broad delivery of the BTS, the DUT is earned in 113 university-based institutes of technology known as IUTs. These institutes offer programmes across the following manufacturing and service specialties.  Chemistry, biology, physics  Electronics, electricity, computer science, mechanics  Public works, energy, security  Administration and business management  Legal and social services  Information technology and communications  Campus France adds, “The range of subjects taught, the selectivity of admissions, the close involvement of economic and social partners, and the excellent employment prospects of graduates make the DUT a programme of excellence recognised throughout the world and chosen by more than 115,000 students every year. E7. Tuition Fees  French higher education, especially in relation to most other leading study destinations, is highly affordable. Tuition fees will vary across public and private institutions but are more stable and predictable in the public sector where most of the cost of education is borne by the French government.  Under current fee schedules, annual tuition fees for most public institutions are as follows.  For students from the European Union:  €170 per year at the   Licence   (bachelor) level  €243 per year at the master level  €601 per year in a school of engineering  €380 per year at the doctoral level  Non-EU students can expect to pay:  €2,770 per year at the   Licence   (bachelor) level  €3,770 per year at the master level  In highly selective institutions and especially at the master’s level, tuition fees will reach as high as €10,000 per year or more. Given the range in tuition levels across the French system, all students are advised to carefully determine the costs of study for their preferred institutions and programmes.  40% COMPLETE     Previous Topic   Next Quiz     10/10/25, 5:02 AM   icef.com/academy/topic/e7-tuition-fees/  https://www.icef.com/academy/topic/e7-tuition-fees/   1/2
